title: Introduction to Lists
description: Store multiple items in one variable.

slides.title: Collections of Data 📦
slides.content:
# Why Lists?

Storing many items individually is messy:
```python
item1 = "apple"
item2 = "banana"
item3 = "cherry"
```

**Lists** store multiple items together:
```python
items = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
```

slides.title: Creating Lists 📝
slides.content:
# List Syntax

```python
# Empty list
empty = []

# With items
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
mixed = [1, "hello", True, 3.14]
```

Lists use square brackets `[]` and commas!

slides.title: Creating Lists 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
# Different types of lists
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40]
mixed = [1, "hello", True]

print("Fruits:", fruits)
print("Numbers:", numbers)
print("Mixed:", mixed)
print("Length:", len(fruits))

slides.title: Accessing Items 🔍
slides.content:
# Indexing

Access items by their position (0-based):

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print(fruits[0])   # "apple"
print(fruits[1])   # "banana"
print(fruits[-1])  # "cherry" (last item)
```

slides.title: List Indexing 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
colors = ["red", "green", "blue", "yellow"]

print("First:", colors[0])
print("Third:", colors[2])
print("Last:", colors[-1])
print("Second to last:", colors[-2])

slides.title: Slicing Lists ✂️
slides.content:
# Get a Portion

```python
nums = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(nums[1:4])   # [1, 2, 3]
print(nums[:3])    # [0, 1, 2]
print(nums[2:])    # [2, 3, 4, 5]
print(nums[::2])   # [0, 2, 4] (step)
```

slides.title: Slicing Examples 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
letters = ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f']

print("Original:", letters)
print("[1:4]:", letters[1:4])
print("[:3]:", letters[:3])
print("[3:]:", letters[3:])
print("[::2]:", letters[::2])
print("Reversed:", letters[::-1])

quizData.question: What does [::-1] do?
quizData.explanation: [::-1] reverses the list by stepping backwards!

slides.title: Lists Are Mutable 🔄
slides.content:
# You Can Change Lists!

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
fruits[1] = "blueberry"  # Change item
print(fruits)  # ["apple", "blueberry", "cherry"]
```

Unlike strings, lists can be modified in place!

slides.title: Modifying Lists 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
scores = [85, 90, 78, 92]
print("Original:", scores)

scores[2] = 88  # Change one
print("After change:", scores)

scores[0:2] = [95, 95]  # Change multiple
print("After slice:", scores)

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## List vs String

| Feature | List | String |
|---------|------|--------|
| Mutable | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Brackets | `[]` | `""` or `''` |
| Items | Any type | Characters |
| Example | `[1, 2, 3]` | `"abc"` |

quizData.question: Are lists mutable?
quizData.explanation: Lists are mutable — you can add, remove, and change items!
